matches.match_rationale: The core deficit is the absence of written learning outcome statements and competency matrices — artifacts that don't yet exist in text form. AI text generation can draft precise, measurable outcome statements (e.g. Bloom's-taxonomy-aligned) and competency descriptors for each program level. This directly addresses the pain mechanism: teachers lack clarity because no authoritative text document defines what students must be able to do.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A tool that ingests the operator's government curriculum frameworks and generates a full internal competency matrix — program by program, year level by year level — with editable outcome statements ready for educator review and network ratification.

matches.match_rationale: Government curriculum documents contain implicit relationships between standards, year levels, and competency domains that are never made explicit in internal planning. Knowledge extraction can surface these relationships — mapping which government standards align to which competency clusters — giving curriculum leaders a structured foundation to build their internal framework on. This directly attacks the 'mapped to government frameworks but not to internal competency targets' gap.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An extraction pipeline that reads all government framework PDFs and outputs a structured competency ontology — subject × year-level × skill-domain — that the operator can then annotate and adopt as their internal standard.

matches.match_rationale: Teachers and curriculum coordinators need to answer specific questions like 'what should a Year 8 student be able to do in persuasive writing?' but no internal document currently provides that answer. A RAG system over existing government frameworks, the operator's own documents, and best-practice curriculum libraries can generate grounded, citation-backed answers. This gives teachers on-demand clarity without waiting for a central curriculum team to produce a finished framework.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A curriculum Q&A assistant that answers teacher queries about expected student competencies by synthesizing government standards, internal documents, and exemplar rubrics — surfacing cited evidence rather than generic advice.

matches.match_rationale: Building a network-wide competency framework is itself a multi-step workflow: gather existing government standards documents, extract competency dimensions, cross-reference against current curriculum scope-and-sequence documents, identify gaps, draft missing outcome statements, and route drafts for review. An automation agent can orchestrate this pipeline across all campuses' inputs, replacing a manual curriculum-director project that typically stalls due to resource constraints.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An agent that ingests each campus's existing curriculum maps, cross-references them against government frameworks and a target competency taxonomy, identifies gaps, generates draft outcome statements to fill those gaps, and produces a consolidated review-ready competency matrix for network sign-off.
